query: As of July 2026, has any U.S. Supreme Court justice announced retirement or resignation? Status of Alito, Thomas retirement speculation after NPR retracted its June 30 2026 story. Any current plans for a justice to retire in summer 2026.
answer:
## Summary

**As of July 2026, no U.S. Supreme Court justice has announced retirement or resignation.** All nine justices remain on the bench.

**The NPR June 30, 2026 story:** NPR erroneously reported that Justice Alito was retiring on the final day of the Court's term. Correspondent Nina Totenberg misheard "retirement announcements" (plural/general) as a singular announcement about Alito while leaving the courthouse. The story was live only ~5-6 minutes (10:51–10:57 a.m. ET) before retraction, with an on-air correction at 11:07 a.m. NPR's executive editor apologized, the public editor published a full explanatory column, Totenberg called it a "rookie mistake" and apologized to Alito, and the Supreme Court's Public Information Office publicly denied the report.

**Alito/Thomas retirement speculation:** Speculation centered on the two oldest conservative justices — Alito (76, on bench since 2006) and Thomas (mid/late 70s, longest-serving since 1991) — driven by the political incentive for Trump to seat younger replacements before the November 2026 midterms. It intensified after Alito's March 20, 2026 hospitalization. However, **the current trajectory points away from a near-term retirement:** ABC News and CBS News reported (April–May 2026, sourced to people close to the justices) that neither plans to retire in 2026. Alito intends to serve into at least 2027 and has been hiring clerks for the 2026–2027 term; Thomas does not plan to step down.

**No credible report of any actual retirement or resignation announcement was found through mid-July 2026.** Full report, sources, and data points delivered via the structured output.

key_facts: As of July 2026, NO sitting U.S. Supreme Court justice has announced retirement or resignation; all nine remain on the bench.
NPR's June 30, 2026 report that Justice Samuel Alito was retiring was erroneous and retracted within ~5-6 minutes; the story was live ~10:51-10:57 a.m. ET, with an on-air correction at 11:07 a.m.

The error came from correspondent Nina Totenberg mishearing 'retirement announcements' (plural/general) as a singular announcement about Alito; she called it a 'rookie mistake' and apologized to Alito.
The Supreme Court's Public Information Office publicly denied the NPR report; NPR's executive editor apologized and its public editor published a full explanatory column.
Retirement speculation centers on Alito (age 76, on bench since 2006) and Clarence Thomas (mid/late 70s, on bench since 1991), the two oldest conservative justices.
Speculation intensified after Alito was hospitalized March 20, 2026 'out of an abundance of caution.'
In April-May 2026, ABC News and CBS News reported (sources close to the justices) that neither Alito nor Thomas plans to retire in 2026.
Alito intends to serve into at least 2027 and has been hiring law clerks for the 2026-2027 term, signaling intent to remain.
Sources close to Clarence Thomas said he does not plan to step down.

Political context: replacing both Alito and Thomas with his own appointees would make Trump the first president since Eisenhower to personally appoint a Supreme Court majority; current reporting indicates Trump is not expected to get a fourth nomination before the November 2026 midterms.

data_points.metric: Sitting Supreme Court justices who have announced retirement/resignation as of July 2026
Approximate minutes NPR's erroneous Alito retirement story was live before retraction
Earliest year Alito intends to continue serving through (per sources)
